data.title: Alpine Income Property: Q3 Earnings Beats Assessed
data.description:
Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (NYSE:PINE) released its third-quarter earnings results on October 17th, revealing a comprehensive earnings target beat, leading to higher full-year guidance.

We've covered Alpine on numerous occasions, the last being in August, when we deemed the REIT a buy. Our thesis argued that Alpine would benefit from the systematic environment and enhanced unit economics. We stand by our historical consensus, yet we decided to update our thesis after the REIT's Q3 earnings release.

Herewith is a summary of our latest findings.

Headline Earnings

Alpine Income Property Trust's Q3 earnings report revealed funds from operations per share of $0.45, beating estimates by five cents. Moreover, the REIT delivered $13.48M in quarterly revenue, a 16.6% year-over-year increase and a target beat of $1.32 million.

The vehicle's robust earnings report led to upgraded guidance. Alpine stated that it anticipates $1.62 per diluted share for 2024, a two-cent upgrade. In addition, the REIT upgraded its adjusted funds from operations guidance to $1.71 from a previous $1.69.

Alpine's earnings beat means it has surpassed four of its quarterly FFO targets in the past two years; however, it has succumbed to four defeats. This suggests its prospective fundamental performance is in the balance.

Details & Our Take

Alpine's key portfolio data shows a one-basis-point decrease in occupancy. However, the REIT's average rent per square foot increased to $11.67 versus sub $11 in Q2, which shows solid growth.

Furthermore, investment-grade base rent dropped by 15%, while its weighted average lease term increased by 1.2 to 8.8 years. The prior's influence is debatable, as non-IG tenants can escalate rental yield yet enhance the REIT's counterparty risk. However, the latter seems net positive, as longer lease terms smooth the vehicle's inflows, making it easier to structure liabilities.

Aside: See our previous article for Q2's data.

Let's delve into a few idiosyncrasies to make sense of the above.

Alpine's lease income increased by about 2.4% year-over-year. We anticipate its lease income to resume solid growth amid the fund's long-term lease agreements and continuous acquisitions. Some REITs might face headwinds from a competitive new lease market, but Alpine's long-term lease structure reduces its market-to-market risk.

Aside: I refer to the new lease market as more competitive, as disinflation might dilute mark-to-market rates.

Furthermore, the REIT experienced a noteworthy increase in income from its loan book, generating $1.663 million in quarterly revenue versus $112,000 a year earlier. As dispensed in our previous article, Alpine engaged in a commercial loan venture by investing in a "one-first mortgage construction loan" of $6.1 million loan with a yield of 11.5%. Alpine extended its loan book in Q2 by purchasing and amending "a first mortgage construction loan, secured by a Publix-anchored shopping center and three out parcels, with a total funding commitment of $17.8 million, of which $10.0 million was funded at closing, at an initial interest rate of 10.25%."

Will Alpine's debt portfolio add value? We believe it might diversify and smooth the REIT's income. In addition, fixed-income investments can assist with the REIT's liability matching.

Let's revise Alpine's cost structure.

Alpine's Q2 accounting-based operating expenses dropped year-on-year, settling at 77.64% of revenue versus 1.1x a year earlier. The primary differentiator was lower impairment provisions, a trend we anticipate to resume as U.S. disinflation has decelerated.

Furthermore, real estate expenses and SG&A increased year over year, yet we attribute this to the growing portfolio size. We anticipate similar throughout-the-cycle line items leading forward.

Lastly, interest expenses settled at around 23.5% on a common size basis, 21.11% a year earlier. Lower federal funds rates, lower SOFR, and tight credit spreads are evident, allowing Alpine to decrease its financing cost within the following quarters, especially as the vehicle's net debt-to-EBITDA improved in the past quarter.

Here's a diagram showing the REIT's debt structure.

Adding to the above, here's a table showing Alpine's salient coverage ratios.

Portfolio Review

Tenant Review

Alpine's portfolio shows that 52% of its base rent derives from investment-grade companies, with DICK'S Sporting Goods (DKS), Walgreens (WBA), Lowe's (LOW), Walmart (WMT), and Best Buy (BBY) being noteworthy tenants.

The REIT's broad-based and investment-grade focus shows that much of its tenant base operates in the consumer products sector. Nonetheless, there are end-market differentiators within, meaning concentration risk probably isn't as noteworthy as many might conclude.

We are slightly concerned by Alpine's non-investment grade exposure. The shift can bolster its adjusted funds from operations growth multiple. Nonetheless, cyclical economic downturns will likely exacerbate counterparty risk, raising an argument for impairment and vacancy risks.

Valuation and Dividends

Fundamental Metrics

Alpine's midpoint 2024 guidance, combined with its October 18 pre-market price, was used to compute its forward price-to-FFO and price-to-AFFO ratios. Additionally, the REIT's dividend yield was drawn from Seeking Alpha's database.

The table below dispenses the calculated figures; a discussion follows.

Metric Value
P/AFFO 10.39x
P/FFO 10.52x
D.Yield 6.34%

Source: Author's Calculations, Data From Alpine and Seeking Alpha.

We deem Alpine's multiples favorable. Its forward P/FFO, which is a commonly used price multiple in REIT analysis, is below the sector median of 14.93x. Secondly, the REIT's forward AFFO ratio, which represents a more economic assessment than FFO, is below the sector average of 17.23x

The abovementioned plays into a broader story. I've reinserted the REIT's peer-based AFFO growth below to illustrate why the REIT's compressed P/FFO and P/AFFO ratios are particularly important.

By comparing Alpine to its self-selected peer group, it is shown that its dividend yield stretched beyond its peer group. Aside from possessing a forward yield of around 6.84%, Alpine's trailing and four-year average yields exceed the pack, providing a telling sign to those seeking income.

REIT TTM D.Yield 4Y AVG D.Yield
Alpine Income 5.98% 5.92%
Essential Properties (EPRT) 3.37% 4.23%
NETSREIT (NTST) 4.98% 3.84%
Agree Realty (ADC) 3.95% 4.19%
Realty Income (O) 4.81% 4.72%
Four Corners (FCPT) 4.64% 5%
NNN REIT (NNN) 4.64% 5.02%

Source: Seeking Alpha

Headwinds

I outlined a few risks throughout the article, but let's consolidate them in a discreet section while adding a few more observations.

While the U.S. economy remains robust, we've experienced a cooling period whereby disinflation, softer consumer utility, and higher interest rates have devalued commercial assets. Moreover, mark-to-market rents have battled amid a more competitive new lease environment. Although we argue that such headwinds have settled, key economic indicators suggest reason to remain wary.

I mentioned that commercial property values have slipped year-on-year. The following diagram communicates that fact. In addition to cyclical pricing risks, we highlight structural risks within the retail space. What do I mean by that? The shift to online sales may cause many retailers to downsize or avoid a brick-and-mortar presence. Thus, a demand-side factor could occur if technological innovation has its say.

Lastly, a financial markets-based view shows that Alpine's Sharpe Ratio and 5% Monthly Value-at-Risk exceed those of the iShares Global REIT ETF (REET). Although we don't consider its risk attribution overly risky, we highlight its relative risk-return metrics as a risk factor.

Final Word

Alpine Income Property's latest earnings beat folds into a broader growth story characterized by scintillating funds from operations growth. Although this REIT faces risks like a competitive new lease market and portfolio concentration, we see a solid opportunity as its headline metrics remain best-in-class. Moreover, we believe Alpine's valuation and dividend metrics set it apart, especially if its impressive unit economics are considered.

Consensus: Market Outperform.

data.title: India Fund: This 10% Yielder Is Still Worth A Look
data.description:
After a very strong year, Indian stocks have finally entered consolidation mode. Partly due to external factors like geopolitics in the Middle East, which has prompted an oil-driven recalibration of earnings expectations. Foreign funds rotating into China haven't helped either. But ultimately, India is a domestic-oriented story, and with the economy potentially entering a soft patch amid spiking food inflation and below-par August industrial production numbers, it makes sense that stocks have pulled back in tandem.

Yet, there remains reason for optimism. For starters, rural demand is finally on the upswing after a much better monsoon season this year. Meanwhile, better-than-expected state election results for the governing coalition mean a much lower likelihood of more capital tax hikes at the next Budget. Perhaps most importantly, large-cap earnings growth is still trending very strongly in the high-teens % for next year (vs. low-teens % this year and >30% last year), more than justifying the >20x forward P/E multiple.

Traditionally, the actively managed The India Fund (NYSE:IFN) has been a great defensive vehicle to ride out the turbulence. Even after the latest set of portfolio changes, an uncertain near-term backdrop should still play into the strengths of this value-focused manager. So should the 'Modi 3.0' backdrop of higher taxes and wider tracking errors for passive ETFs (see India Fund: A Most Interesting Large-Cap India Play). Heading into its year-end distribution, IFN's high-single-digit % NAV discount offers investors an interesting entry point into this ~10% yielder.

IFN Overview – Fundamentally Status Quo

Indian equities aren't as 'hot' as they were post-election, so it’s perhaps not too surprising that the closed-end India Fund has seen relatively modest asset growth this time around (up to ~$786m vs ~$778m last quarter).

Elsewhere, the 1.43% gross expense ratio means IFN comes with a slight fee premium to key comparable, Morgan Stanley's India Investment Fund (IIF), which charges ~1.38%. IFN also retains a big liquidity advantage, though, so after factoring in its execution advantage, the fund still has a slight cost edge.

India Fund

India Investment Fund

Assets (USD' m)

786

330

Expense Ratio

1.43%

1.38% (1.33% net of waivers)

Average Volume (10-day)

82.9k

40.0k

Source: abrdn, Morgan Stanley, Yahoo Finance

IFN Portfolio – Mind the Growing Small/Mid-Cap Allocation

The India Fund has not been a particularly active fund in the past, nor has it deviated too far from its MSCI India benchmark. This has begun to change, though, with IFN's latest disclosures showing that its 45-stock portfolio skews further toward India's higher growth (but also higher risk) small/mid-caps than before.

September 2024

June 2024

Large-Caps

74.72

77.53

Small/Mid-Caps

24.02

21.57

Source: abrdn

Yes, the top holdings remain blue-chip names like ICICI Bank (IBN) (7.2%), HDFC Bank (HDB) (4.9%), and Tata Consultancy Services (4.8%). But a closer look at its latest holdings disclosure shows that the tail is now a lot heavier on small/mid-caps. Also notable is the fact that IFN has dropped two large-cap names (Maruti Suzuki and Container Corp of India) in favor of two small/mid-caps (Brigade Enterprises and Poly Medicure). In essence, the shift from large-cap-focused to 'all-cap' appears to be a deliberate one.

Inclusions

Size

Exclusion

Size

Smid

Smid

Source: abrdn

While IFN has drifted away from an MSCI India-style allocation, it's worth noting that relative to key US-listed active comparable IIF, this is still the more large-cap-focused active manager.

India Fund

India Investment Fund

Large Cap

74.7%

74.5%

Small/Mid-Cap

24.0%

31.9%

Source: abrdn, Morgan Stanley

Plus, IFN maintains a more 'risk-off' approach to allocation. Firstly, by running a lower net long equity position (~99% vs ~106% for IIF). And secondly, by holding off on the rush to anchor new offerings (unlike IIF (see IIF: Finding New Ways To Outperform)) - despite how well its pre-IPO investment in Bharti Hexacom has turned out.

Bharti Hexacom

Anchor Price

570

Current Price (18th Oct)

1,495.55

Implied Return (%)

162.4%

Source: Chittorgarh

Both decisions will no doubt cost IFN a few points of relative performance in a bull market. On the flip side, the fund also maintains a bit more resilience (vs IIF) in a downturn. Thus, a worthy option for those looking to balance the upside with some downside protection.

IFN Performance – Pacing Well into the Final Stretch

Coming off a bumper 2023, this year is again shaping up well for IFN. Per the fund's latest factsheet, returns are already up to +20.6% (in NAV terms). This is slightly behind MSCI India (+22.8%), but once you factor in capital taxes and other transaction costs associated with investing in India, IFN has really done quite well. Consider that the US-listed MSCI India tracker fund, INDA, arguably IFN's actual benchmark, returned just short of +20% through September 2024.

Of course, IFN is a closed-end fund and quite a unique one at that, since it periodically issues shares. The rights issue (i.e., granting additional shares at a discount to market) earlier this year was a case in point and a key reason for the year-to-date volatility in the NAV discount.

Post-rights, the market has settled on a ~6% discount to NAV. At first glance, this might seem unwarranted relative to the other closed end India fund IIF (currently priced at a high-teens % discount). But it’s worth keeping in mind two key differentiators that keep the IFN discount sustainably anchored. Firstly, the Board's commitment to a 10% "rolling distribution rate" (paid quarterly). And secondly, by paying in "newly issued shares of the Fund" at "the lower of the NAV per share or the market price per share with a floor for the NAV of not less than 95%".

Adding to this is the 'optionality' of discounted share issuances (e.g., this year's rights offering), as well as the Board's open-ended commitment to "review possible actions to reduce any such discount." With these in mind, IFN has a stronger case than most to close its NAV discount over time and thus, rightly trades at a tighter NAV-market price spread.

Summing Up

As we enter a new 'Modi 3.0' regime featuring higher capital taxes and wider tracking errors for conventional ETFs, the case for an active manager like IFN is as compelling as it's ever been. While not as defensive as before, this is still a value-focused manager with a lot to offer, especially if we do see market turbulence near-term. Patient investors get paid via a (narrowing) NAV discount and a 10% yield while they sit and wait.

data.title: The Ultimate Portfolio Structure For Stress-Free High Current Income
data.description:
One of the main benefits of devising a durable income focused portfolio strategy is that the entire investing process is de-risked both from goal achievement and mental perspective.

Usually, income investing comes with an absolute goal setting (e.g., growing portfolio income streams by 5% each quarter, receiving $300 more in current income each quarter, developing a portfolio that distributes $5,000 in monthly dividends). Namely, there is no focus on beating the market or capturing a specific level of portfolio price appreciation. Because of this, an income focused strategy is inherently more predictable and puts less pressure on investor nerves by avoiding huge volatility in the goal achievement process.

The chart below reflects the essence very nicely. It shows how over the past 10-year period, one of my favorite income picks, Realty Income Corporation (O) has delivered consistent dividends, while at the same time going through a huge roller-coaster ride.

However, there is one important caveat here. Such kind of strategy of accessing truly durable and stress-free income works only if the companies with strong fundamentals and defensive characteristics are selected.

Thus, for durable current income investors, it is always a challenge to strike a balance between high-yield and low risks.

Let me now explain how I approach (and how I would recommend doing that) the construction of a durable and high dividend income portfolio that could also serve as a basis for retirement income seeking investors.

The approach

Since income investing is at the core about deploying our savings into yield-producing assets, which distribute tangible cash flows so that return targets could be met even if the price does not go up, there has to be an emphasis on high-yielding securities.

Yet, as described above, it is not only about yield, but also about having that yield stable and, preferably, growing above inflation rate to avoid loss of purchasing power.

The thing is that finding high-yielding securities is very easy, and could be done through various publicly available stock screening databases. However, when it comes to making sure that the quality is there, it gets quite complicated.

I have tried to visualize this through (hopefully) easy-to-understand chart below. The chart reflects the process how durable income investors should, in my opinion, approach the asset screening. It all starts with developing a huge sample of high-yielding securities and then step-by-step narrowing the option space by filtering out high and medium risk assets.

What we can also see in the chart is a reflection of the minimum required criteria that durable income investments have to embody in order to mitigate the main risk for this strategy (i.e., experiencing dividend cuts).

Here, I would like to underscore that in most cases, it is definitely worth peeling the onion back a bit more to understand that the underlying fundamentals are really strong across the board. In my personal case, I conduct a very thorough due diligence before investing since the intention is to effectively buy and hold for a truly long period of time.

This time I have also tried to express the main underlying criteria through (hopefully) a visually appealing figure.

Once the qualified investments are identified and there is a list of them that could be included in a portfolio, then the final step is to prioritize and allocate them based on a specific methodology.

The methodology or prioritization framework is obviously different for each investor. As visualized in the process below, in my opinion, we could arrive at some common principles, which should form a core basis for most durable income seeking investor portfolios.

The strategic offense and strategic defense categories would form a backbone of the portfolio. These investments enjoy high-yield and low-risk or low yield and low risk. By saying this, let's keep in mind that we at this point we have already filtered out stocks with excessive risks profiles or with too low yields. So, the comparisons here are relative within the sample of "qualified investments".

The low yield and low-risk category (strategic defense) would have to be comprised of truly defensive names, where the chances of experiencing distribution cuts down the road are as low as possible. The high-yield low-risk category (strategic offense) would be the second-largest portfolio category, where the yields are higher and the odds of dividend reduction remain very small (albeit, not as small as in the strategic defense bucket). The immediate assets that come into mind in the context of this category are BDCs and leveraged CEFs.

Tactical offense and tactical speculation categories would constitute relatively small chunks of the total portfolio exposure. In low yield and high-risk category (tactical offense), there could be investments made into securities that do not necessarily offer abnormal yields, but have a very favorable risk and reward profile due to temporary dislocations in the market or deeply depressed valuations, which are unjustified given solid fundamentals and stable business. The high-yield and high-risk category (tactical speculation) is about deploying capital into, usually, double-digit yielding securities, which have indeed a chance of registering a cut, but this chance is not that high or even if the cut was made, the yield would still remain fairly attractive.

The sample portfolio allocations

In the sample portfolio allocation structure below, I will present three examples of what could be core holdings in the strategic defense and offense bucket. For tactical offense and speculation, I will offer one pick for each, just to illustrate the essence.

Strategic defense

As elaborated above, strategic defense is about maximizing the defense within a pre-selected high yielding and relatively safe security universe. For this category, I would dedicate 40% to 60% of the total portfolio exposure.

I would certainly include here Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Enbridge (ENB), and Realty Income Corporation.

All three of these carry investment grade balance sheets and have well-laddered debt financing profiles, which help mitigate the refinancing risk. In fact, EPD and O have upper investment grade credit ratings, which are indicative of even stronger credit profiles.

The business models for ENB and EPD are inherently stable, as they are underpinned by cost of service agreements by bankable offtakers / users for the company midstream infrastructure assets. The beauty here is that the majority of these agreements are linked to CPI dynamics, which provide an element of decent organic growth. The same applies for O, which has ~ 9.7 years of weighted average lease agreement profile, which is backed by diversified set of customers (in durable retail space). The lease agreements come also with attached CPI or fixed growth annual escalators.

All of the three businesses are able to reinvest back in the organic or M&A growth by heavily relying on retained cash flow generation (after dividend distributions and debt repayments). EPD is the best example in this context, having 190% distribution coverage ratio, even though it currently offers 7.2% yield.

Strategic offense

This portfolio bucket is focused on capturing higher yields than in the strategic defense case. The risks are indeed higher, but still relatively low, with no meaningful probabilities of dividend cuts. In this bucket, I would concentrate 20% to 30% of the durable income seeking portfolio exposure.

The names, which, in my opinion, fit nicely into the strategic offense bucket are Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC), and PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO). The current yields are higher than in the previous bucket. BXSL, JPC and PDO yield 10.1%, 9.9% and 11.0%, respectively.

The beauty here is that all three picks carry very limited risk of suddenly announcing a dividend cut. BXSL could be deemed as one of the highest quality BDCs out there with consistently low non-accruals, senior secured first lien focus and ~ 95% of the income stemming from well-capitalized and strong cash flowing businesses (e.g., the newly funded investments in Q2, 2024 had a weighted average LTV of only 37.9%).

JPC and PDO are leveraged CEF vehicles, which are underpinned by fixed income instruments that already per definition introduce stronger cash flow predictability than, say, pure play equity exposures. In JPC's case, we are talking about focus on preferred securities from which the majority are classified as investment grade (and 12% of the portfolio are even placed in the upper investment grade category). In PDO's case, the lion's share of the investments is in mortgage backed securities and U.S. Government related fixed income instruments. Compared to JPC, PDO has an even higher duration profile, which makes it more sensitive to interest rate changes. In the current environment, where we have a downward-sloping FOMC dot plot curve (interest rate curve), this could provide a significant boost at the asset price level as well.

Tactical speculation

The tactical speculation is the highest risk and highest yield segment, which should not constitute a significant part of the portfolio. This portfolio bucket should consume about 5% to 10% of the total exposure, where the allocations, which comprise the bucket, should preferably be diversified as much as possible.

In this context, my favorite pick is FS KKR Capital (FSK), which is one of the largest BDCs with a current yield of ~ 13.9%. As of now, it trades at 15% discount to NAV mostly because of the historical non-accruals, that have several times surprised the investors to the downside. Up until Q1, 2024, FSK quite consistently registered sizable write-downs due to a weak asset base, which was diluted by an unsuccessful external portfolio acquisition several years ago. However, now things have finally changed (i.e., the portfolio clean-up process is largely completed) and the past two quarters have been characterized by stable performance. On top of this, the leverage has dropped below sector average, while the credit quality of the investment positions has improved (e.g., weighted average EBITDA has grown by ~ 12% on a year-over-year basis). All in all, FSK presents a very attractive risk and return profile, offering investors a massive yield, while waiting for the thesis to play out.

Tactical offense

The final portfolio bucket is tactical offense, where the risks are higher than in the strategic allocation buckets, and the return potential lies in the combination of acceptable yield and notable price appreciation. The optimal portfolio weights would be also somewhere between 5% to 10%.

Here, one of my favorite picks would be Global Medical (GMRE), which is a net lease equity REITs operating in the medical office building segment. It trades at P/FFO of 11.7x and offers a dividend yield of 8.8%.

The reason why GMRE trades at so depressed multiple is related to largely exhausted AFFO payout ratio (~ 90%), and risks that are associated with properties being located in tertiary markets. Plus, there is also a refinancing risk involved, which could theoretically, if interest rates do not decline, impose additional challenges on the dividend coverage front.

Yet, the case, in my opinion, is nicely de-risked due to clearly dropping base rates in combination with GMRE's proven ability to monetize its assets at low cap rates (around 6%). The received proceeds from both property monetization and organic growth, which stems from periodic rent escalators offer a well-needed capital for GMRE to bring down debt and leave some liquidity to acquire new properties at much higher cap rates (at ~8% based on Q1 and Q2, 2024 data points). Given this, the chances of a dividend cut are limited, and the possibility for experiencing a healthy multiple recovery is quite high, especially against the backdrop of falling interest rates.

The bottom line

In this article, I have outlined a detailed approach on how high and durable income seeking investors could potentially go about structuring their portfolios. The ultimate objective with this kind of portfolio (or approach) is two-fold: 1) maximize current income, and 2) minimize income cut risk. The price appreciation element is not that important.

I hope that the approach from security selection to portfolio allocations that are categorized in four separate buckets: strategic defense, strategic offense, tactical speculation, and tactical offense provide you with a decent basis for inspiration on how to devise your durable income portfolio strategy. Finally, the highlighted securities above could also offer some practical ideas (in all cases I have bullish views on those investments) on what kind of securities fit under each of the four portfolio buckets.

data.title: Mastercard: Differences From Visa Are Critical To Understand
data.description:
Mastercard (NYSE:MA) is set to report its third-quarter results in less than two weeks, amid a year of slight underperformance for the payments giant.

The upcoming quarter will be key in determining whether the premium over Visa (V) remains justified, and could mark a turning point for my three-pillar growth thesis.

Let's dive in.

Introduction

I've been covering both Mastercard and Visa across a series of articles in the past two years.

Throughout the period, I maintained a 'Buy' rating for both companies, and preferred Visa over Mastercard.

So far, I'm sad to say I was wrong, as both Mastercard and Visa lagged the market during this time, and Mastercard significantly outperformed Visa. The DoJ's lawsuit against Visa's debit business played a big role here, but it's not entirely to blame.

Still, I might sound stubborn, but I think it's one of those situations where I can say my analysis was correct, while the outcome was not.

This is where we should go back to my three-pillar thesis, but first we should go over the key differences between the companies.

Key Differences Between Visa And Mastercard

Let's make it clear, Mastercard and Visa have nearly identical businesses. Even in this new era, they structured their companies somewhat similarly, across consumer payments, value-added services, and new flows.

That said, there are several key differences between them which make a big difference, in my view, when looking at their next growth phase, which should be primarily driven by the non-consumer pillars.

The first major difference is their size. Even after years of market share gains, Mastercard is still significantly smaller than Visa, with the latter generating ~30% more revenues, and facilitating 65% more volume.

The second difference is their geographic exposure. Last quarter, Mastercard generated around 45% of its revenues in the Americas region (primarily U.S.), whereas Visa was at 41%, for the U.S. alone. However, on volumes, Mastercard is at 67% outside the U.S., whereas Visa is at 54%. Historically, Mastercard is materially more exposed to international markets compared to Visa.

The last difference comes down to client incentives, and profitability. In an effort to continuously take market share from Visa, Mastercard is sacrificing profitability in order to acquire customers. Its client incentives as a percentage of gross revenues are typically 10 points higher than Visa's, and as a result, its operating margins are about 10 points lower.

So, summing up this section, Visa is larger, more exposed to the U.S., and less aggressive on customer acquisition, making it more profitable.

The Three-Pillar Thesis

Mastercard and Visa are still viewed by many as pure plays on digital consumer payments. However, this is not really the case anymore.

Last quarter, Mastercard generated nearly 40% of its revenues from what it defines as value-added services, while for Visa this figure stood at around 25%.

For both companies, this portion of the business is growing much faster than consumer payments. In Mastercard's case, VAS grew 18% last quarter, compared to the consolidated 13%, and in Visa's case, it was 23% and 10%.

When it comes to New Flows, they don't disclose the size of the business and Mastercard doesn't constantly share its growth, but for Visa, it grew 18% last quarter.

What I'm trying to emphasize here is that in the midterm, those non-consumer pillars will become increasingly important. Actually, I argue they will become the focus points for investors, and it won't take too long before they generate more revenues than the consumer payments segment.

This should become even more apparent when the conversions of Citizens and Fifth Third Banks are complete. These conversions are the primary drivers for Mastercard's higher volume growth, which is currently the main topic in Wall Street analysts' reports.

Under such scenario, I find Mastercard a bit worse positioned. Those new pillars rely heavily on existing relationships (which Visa has more of), and like many innovative services and products, they are more likely to first be adopted in the U.S. (where Visa has more exposure).

As I wrote in the past, I believe the market is missing the size of this opportunity (when it comes to both companies' valuations), but also its significance (when it comes to Mastercard's premium valuation over Visa).

In the upcoming quarter, I expect the market to remain focused on the legacy businesses and the growth gap there, but I will be on the other side of that, closely monitoring the relative performance in value-added services and new flows. I'll also focus on the client incentives gap, which the market seems to be ignoring.

Valuation & Outlook

Mastercard is expected to outgrow Visa for the foreseeable future, with consensus estimates at 15%-17% EPS growth and 10%-12% revenue growth for the upcoming years (Visa is at 12%-14% and 9%-11%, respectively).

Mastercard is currently trading at roughly a 20% premium on 2025 estimates, while being expected to grow about 2 percentage points faster. To me, such high premium could only be justified by the lower regulatory risk.

Further, at 31 times '25 estimates, Mastercard is close to being fully valued in my view, trading only 5% below its historical average.

Although I'm very bullish on the new growth pillars, the legacy consumer payments business demands a higher multiple in my view. It has a wider moat, and its underlying secular tailwinds (global GDP per capita and digitalization) are more resilient as well.

That's why I don't think Mastercard is expected to see its multiple expand materially, which means returns will be driven by EPS growth (still great).

Conclusion

Mastercard is an extraordinary business with a long runway for mid-teens EPS growth.

Because of the key differences between Mastercard and Visa, I believe the latter is slightly better positioned to capitalize on the growth opportunities outside of consumer payments.

Although Mastercard's premium can be justified by lower regulatory risk and faster growth, I think its 31x multiple on 2025 earnings is close to peak.

This means that investors should expect shares to follow earnings growth, which is still great, as it means ~15% annual returns in the foreseeable future.

Therefore, I reiterate Mastercard at a 'Buy'.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 10/20/24
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.
