data.title: Pernod Ricard: Chinese Tariffs Threaten Long-Term Growth
data.description:
It is not an easy time for all companies that sell spirits, in fact, their sales are struggling to grow, especially in China. Pernod Ricard's (OTCPK:PRNDY) (OTCPK:PDRDF) quarterly report was recently released, and its results were unable to lift the situation.

The French company, among the world's leading sellers of wines and spirits, is facing one of the most difficult periods in its recent history. The long-term trend remains positive, but over the past year and a half the price per share has suffered a major setback. In addition, some regulations are worrying shareholders.

Highlights Q1 FY25

Total sales reached €2.78 billion, down 8.50% from last year and 5.90% on an organic basis. The most distressed geographical areas are the U.S., where sales plummeted by 10%, and China.

On China, the discussion needs to be deepened since sales did not drop by a little, but by 26%, a figure that worried shareholders a lot. The causes behind this result are many, and some undermine long-term growth:

  • First, the trade war between China and Europe is leading to an escalation where many companies will be weakened, and among them European brandy producers such as PRNDY. In fact, EU brandy importers will have to pay a tax of up to 39% (already in effect as of Friday, October 11). This move by the Chinese government came a week after the European Union's decision to tax Chinese electric vehicle imports to a greater extent.
  • Secondly, China is facing a complex period because of the crisis that resulted from the bursting of the real estate bubble. Chinese households can no longer spend as much as before, and this weakening is plaguing many foreign companies selling in this country.

So, real estate crisis and rising tariffs have completely annihilated the growth of PRNDY in this country. The problem is that we don't know how long this difficult time will last and what the consequences of tariffs will actually be:

So, the impact of tariffs, it's obviously early stage. We have some estimates for an annualized amount. We are working already to obviously reduce the net impact on our performance as soon as this year. When it comes to passing the tariff as price increases, let me say that it's much too early and I cannot give you specific responses in terms of what we're going to do to tariff. We will adapt, that's for sure.

Passing on the tariff as price increase is one option. Again, very sensitive, we need as well to monitor what the competitor will be doing. And to be fair, the market conditions are not conducive to large price increase.

EVP, Finance Hélène de Tissot, conference call Q1 FY25

The company is still undecided whether to raise the price of products to cope with tariffs or not. On the one hand, this move could save profit margins, but on the other hand, sales volume is in danger of collapsing too much. After all, China is not experiencing economic expansion. Moreover, if PRNDY shifts the burden of tariffs to consumers and competitors do not, it risks losing significant market share in a key geographic area. In short, PRNDY is between the anvil and the hammer, which is why the price per share shows no signs of life.

To make matters worse, the U.S. is also proving to be a difficult market in which to operate: sales are down 10%. Investors are legitimately concerned since China and the U.S. are the two most important markets for PRNDY.

And China is in this situation, which we expect to persist during the year. That's why for the full year, we expect a more significant decline in China than the initial estimate. It doesn't mean that we expect China to be at same trend than it is in Q1, to be fair.

So then for the other markets, U.S., the expectation for the full year is that the net sales are likely to be in decline, but with a gradual improvement in the underlying sell-out in a market which is still normalizing, but with some inventory adjustments. So, to be clear, it's expected to be better than Q1.

EVP, Finance Hélène de Tissot, conference call Q1 FY25

As icing on the cake, Europe is also disappointing: sales are down 3%. According to management, adverse summer weather conditions were a contributing factor. Japan and especially India were the only positive notes, with the Royal Stag, Blenders Pride, and Jameson brands growing double digits in this area.

Dividends and valuation ratios

Since the all-time high, the stock has collapsed more than 40%, so PRNDY is much cheaper than a few years ago. The current GAAP P/E (FWD) is 17.73x, while the 5-year average is 22.23x, so PRNDY seems undervalued relative to the historical multiple. Multiplying the latter by current expected EPS in 2025 ($1.61), fair value would amount to $35.79 per share, a potential upside of 29%.

Comparing PRNDY with competitors, again, it is the cheapest company in terms of multiples. In any case, this does not mean that it is the best investment of all, as the market may have priced it lower since it is more distressed than competitors: profitability also matters.

In terms of net income margin, PRNDY is inferior to both Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.B) and Diageo (DEO), and slightly superior to Davide Campari (OTCPK:DVCMY). In terms of Levered FCF margin, PRNDY is superior only to Davide Campari.

Thus, PRNDY is cheaper on a multiples basis, but it is explained by worse profitability than peers.

The stock has fallen a lot and the potential for recovery is there, after all, PRNDY remains one of the biggest players in this sector, but the risks should not be underestimated. Chinese tariffs threaten long-term growth, and we do not know if there will be new ones in the future. The trade war between the West and China will favor some companies but disadvantage others. To date, PRNDY is not benefiting.

Of all the companies in this industry, I find BF.B to be the most interesting, as it has excellent profitability and reasonable multiples, a combination that I really appreciate. So, if I had to choose one among them, it would not be PRNDY.

Finally, one last important aspect is the dividend matter. The sharp drop in the price per share has caused the dividend yield (TTM) to skyrocket to 3.93%, not bad for a solid consumer staples. However, don't underestimate these two issues:

  • The first is that the Cash Dividend Payout Ratio (TTM) has reached 126%, which means that in the coming months the company may decide to suspend dividend growth or reduce it.
  • The second is that investing in a French company for dividends is not convenient because of a tax issue. If you do not live in France, you will have to pay a withholding tax of 12.80% and then your national dividend tax.

Overall, double taxation and dubious sustainability are two factors to consider if you are interested in PRNDY dividends.

Conclusion

Pernod Ricard is a French company that has dominated the world wine and spirits industry for decades. There are not many companies competing with it, which is a sign that its industry has significant barriers to entry.

In itself, the company is solid, but it is going through a very complex phase because of the sharp decline in China sales. We do not yet know what impact the tariffs will have, but we do know that the Chinese real estate crisis will continue to be a burden in FY2025. India and Japan are two growth areas, but they alone cannot offset the negative results in the U.S., Europe, and China.

Its valuation multiples are quite low compared to the historical average, suggesting a possible undervaluation. Based on expected EPS in FY2025, this company should be trading at a price 29% higher than current levels, but it is wise not to underestimate the risks. In addition, other companies such as Brown-Forman seem more ready to deal with the current macroeconomic environment: its multiples are reasonable, and its profitability is great.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ASML Holding: The Bottom May Be Already In, Contrarian Buy
data.description:
Investors in ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) got some bad news this week after the chip-machine manufacturer reported worse-than-anticipated results for its third quarter and curtailed its sales forecast for 2025. The company’s stock plunged 16% on its first day, and another 6% the second day after earnings.

As a matter of fact, the shock was so profound that the entire chip sector declined, resulting in mounting pressure even for a company like NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA). ASML Holding, which is a bellwether in the semiconductor industry, is seeing slowing demand for some of its lithography machines, which has been interpreted by investors to mean that the semiconductor sector as a whole is in trouble.

Given the historically volatile pattern in bookings, however, investors are dealing with a contrarian buying opportunity in my view. ASML Holding is more cheaply valued after the consolidation, and the sudden change in sentiment indicates that the market may be overreacting.

ASML Holding - Investors Panic Over Volatile Net System Bookings

ASML produces and sells extreme ultraviolet lithography machines that are used in the chip-manufacturing process. In simple terms, companies like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) need ASML’s chip-making machines to produce microchips that are being used in everything ranging from industrial appliances to electric-vehicles. In addition, ASML Holding sells computational lithography software and generally benefits from growing investments in future AI chip capacity.

In recent days, ASML Holding has come under pressure, mainly after the Netherlands and the U.S. implemented export restrictions for the kind of ultraviolet EUV lithography machines that ASML Holding is selling. According to new these new restrictions, the company will have to apply for export licenses for its TWINSCAN NXT: 1970i and 1980i DUV immersion lithography systems. China accounted for 47% of net system sales in the third quarter, so the country is an important market for ASML Holding.

Additionally, ASML Holding’s third quarter earnings disappointed investors in terms of net system bookings, which is a key figure that indicates future sales potential.

Third quarter net sales of ASML Holding amounted to 7.5 billion euros, reflecting a jump of 20% compared to the previous quarter, and the sales figure surpassed the Wall Street estimate of 7.17 billion euros. Net system sales, which produce the main chunk of sales for ASML Holding, totaled 5.9 billion euros, up 24% QoQ.

ASML Holding gets bookings for its lithography systems in advance and reports net systems bookings activity as a separate number. Obviously, net system bookings are an indicator of leading sales, as companies that place an order will eventually have to pay for it.

In the third quarter, ASML Holding unfortunately experienced a decrease in its net systems bookings: Total 3Q24 bookings amounted to 2,633 million euros, reflecting a decline of 53% QoQ. This decline was explained by management as being the result of a sluggish recovery in the semiconductor industry.

Based on Bloomberg’s compilation of ASML Holding’s historical order volumes, however, I would warn against overreacting to the QoQ drop in net systems bookings, which have historically proven to be highly volatile. For instance, net system bookings for ASML Holding decreased 42% QoQ a year ago, in Q3’23, only to rebound 253% in Q4’23.

What I am saying here is investors should be very careful reading too much into the decline of ASML Holding’s systems bookings in 3Q24, as historical patterns indicate that order volumes are much more volatile than sales and can snap back just as quickly as they decreased.

In addition, ASML Holding is forecasting sales in the amount of 30-35 billion euros in 2025, up from 28 billion euros this year. Put simply, despite a substantial (and probably temporary) decrease in net systems bookings, the ASML Holding is still poised to grow, both in terms of sales and profits. In terms of net sales, they could go up as much as 25% in 2025 (based on the revised forecast) while profits are anticipated to grow even quicker.

Furthermore, I don’t see much concern from a margin picture either. If the bellwether company were to see deteriorating fundamentals, ASML Holding would probably not have seen a growing operating income margin in the third quarter. In 3Q24, the operating income margin totaled 32.7%, up 3.3 percentage points.

ASML Holding Is Cheap

The market models $20.72 per share in profits this year and $28.20 per share next year, which reflects back to us 36% profit growth YoY, which is 9 percentage points higher than its net sales are anticipated to grow in the best scenario.

Put simply, if ASML Holding could grow its net sales to 35 billion euros next year, there is a good chance that this growth will be accompanied by higher operating income margins.

With ASML Holding’s stock selling for $700.60 at the time of writing, the valuation implies a 2024 profit multiple of 33.8x. This multiple falls to 24.8x if next year’s profits are used for the multiple calculation. Investors can now buy the stock at a 37% discount to the July highs (around $1,100) and the stock may have already bottomed following an overly vicious selloff following 3Q24 earnings.

I think ASML Holding could return to a 31x 2025 earnings multiple (the valuation multiple before this week’s selloff) as investors should understand that a quarterly look at order volumes is not that telling and the company’s forecast for 2025 is still quite robust. Thus, I see a return to $875 as a short-term target, based on a 31x leading profit multiple.

A rebound in net systems bookings in 4Q24, as we have seen last year, could drive the stock substantially past this intrinsic value estimate, particularly if ASML Holding managed to grow its operating income margins at the same time.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Not Work Out

ASML Holding might see a contraction in its chip-making machine sales given the latest restrictions on exports to China. With that being said, I think that export restrictions will be eased over time (as they always are), sometimes for political and sometimes for economic reasons.

ASML produces a little less than 20% of its sales from China, so the country is an important destination for its lithography systems. In the case that export restrictions hold, ASML Holding might see, in the worst case, a substantial reduction in sales. Thus, a possible loss of China-related sales is something that I would consider to be the company’s biggest potential headwind.

My Conclusion

I think investors have come completely unglued in the last two days, which I don’t think is either very helpful or constructive.

ASML Holding is a well-managed company selling high-quality chip-making equipment, and the company’s forecast for 2024 implies 28 billion euros in sales and up to 35 billion euros next year.

Profit growth for next year is set to come out to 36% and the stock has become a lot cheaper in the last two days, primarily because investors panicked about a not-very-telling drop in net systems bookings. Historically, order volumes have been highly volatile, and last year bounced back from a similarly high QoQ drop in the fourth quarter.

In addition, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing’s strong results provide much-needed assurance that the semiconductor business is indeed firing on all cylinders. Therefore, I think the selloff in ASML Holding’s stock is a big opportunity for contrarian investors to back up the truck.

With TSM’s results for 3Q24 providing strong support as well, I would not be surprised to see that ASML has already bottomed.

data.title: MSTY: A Monthly Income Machine Fueled By MicroStrategy's Volatility
data.description:
The YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:MSTY) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (ETF) designed to generate high monthly income by utilizing an options strategy centered around MicroStrategy (MSTR) stock. The ETF is part of the YieldMax series, which offers ETFs that actively trade options on large tech stocks.

MSTY, launched in September 2023, offers investors a way to benefit from the volatile prices of MicroStrategy stock without having to trade options themselves. The ETF will actively trade options on MicroStrategy and directly distribute the generated income to its investors. For this service, MSTY charges its holders a 1% expense ratio.

The following section will examine the ETF's performance, holdings, and future outlook.

Investment Strategy

MSTY’s strategy is to generate monthly income by trading options on MicroStrategy stock. Rather than holding shares directly, MSTY simulates a synthetic long position in MSTR by purchasing call options and simultaneously selling put options. Once this synthetic long position is established, the fund sells call options on MSTR stock to generate premium income.

MicroStrategy is known for its aggressive investments in Bitcoin, which makes its stock highly volatile. MSTY takes advantage of MicroStrategy's price swings by selling short-term call options. When the stock's price is volatile, these options can be sold for a higher price, generating potential profits for MSTY investors.

The fund mainly employs 2 strategies:

1) Standard Strategy: Writing Covered Calls on the Synthetic Stock positions. This strategy will benefit from the incoming premium of the sold call options, but at the same time, this caps the upward potential of the synthetic long stock position.

2) Opportunistic Strategy: Credit Call Spreads. This involves selling call options with a lower strike price while buying call options with a higher strike price. This strategy allows the Fund to profit from the difference in option premiums, while also potentially benefiting from an increase in the underlying security's price if it rises above the higher strike price.

To complement its options strategy, MSTY also invests in short-term U.S. Treasuries to earn income on its cash position.

Distribution and Performance

MSTY currently has a distribution rate of 79%, making it an attractive choice for income-seeking investors. This distribution rate is calculated by annualizing the most recent payout and dividing it by the ETF’s share price. The high distribution yield is predominantly due to the high option premiums MSTY collects by selling options on MicroStrategy's volatile stock.

While MSTY generates substantial income, it’s important to note that the upside potential for capital gains is capped due to the call strategies. There is no "free lunch" being given here, the fund gives up the upside potential of a long position in MSTR in exchange for a premium. Thus, if MSTR stock sees a large rally MSTY will only seek out returns less than that of the stock. The trade-off is to provide that more consistent monthly income, which as we know regularly produces less volatility than the route of capital growth (of course this depends on market rates).

Over time, MSTY has proven to be a consistent source of income for investors, thanks to its regular dividend-like distributions.

Holdings

MSTY’s portfolio consists of a mix of options contracts on MicroStrategy stock and U.S. Treasuries. The fund’s managers adjust the strike prices and expiration dates of the call and put options they hold, aiming to maximize the premiums collected while keeping the risk under control. By selling call options, the fund can generate income for its investors, even during periods of flat stock performance.

The fund's current holdings include a synthetic long position in MSTR (see yellow squares) with a strike price of $195, combined with a credit call spread on the $225-$240 strikes maturing on 18 October 2024 (green squares). These positions are consistent with the strategy described in the MSTY prospectus; both the standard strategy and the opportunistic strategy are applied here. The non-utilized cash is invested in US Treasuries (short-term dated ones).

While the accumulation model and options strategies are fairly accessible, an active options trader would likely put on a great deal of commission churn to replicate this strategy in their accounts without paying MSTY 1% per year.

Historical Performance

Since its launch, MSTY has been able to provide regular monthly distributions while mirroring MSTR’s stock movements, albeit with reduced upside potential. MicroStrategy’s stock is known for its large price swings, which offer opportunities to generate option premiums. MicroStrategy’s outsized exposure to Bitcoin largely drives this volatility, as the company has tied a significant portion of its corporate treasury to the cryptocurrency.

MSTY’s performance tends to follow MSTR’s stock movements, though its returns are more muted due to the options strategy. In rising markets, the fund captures less of the upside because the call options sold by the fund limit potential gains. However, this strategy can be beneficial during periods of volatility, as the fund’s income potential increases when option premiums rise due to market uncertainty.

If we look at the price performance of the last 6 months, we can see that MSTY has delivered a 45% ROI, whereas MSTR delivered a 55% return to its investors. The fund did a pretty good job of tracking the yield of MicroStrategy.

Examining MSTR's implied volatility over the past year reveals that it has consistently remained elevated, fluctuating between 50% and 150%. When implied volatility reaches these levels, option prices typically become quite expensive. This phenomenon explains how MSTY can offer such substantial distribution rates to its investors.

The high implied volatility environment creates favorable conditions for MSTY's strategy, as it can capitalize on inflated option premiums to generate significant income. This relationship between market volatility, option pricing, and fund distributions underscores the mechanism behind MSTY's ability to maintain attractive payouts to its shareholders.

Looking forward, we would expect the fund to keep delivering strong distributions to its investors, as we expect the underlying volatility to remain rather high. MicroStrategy currently has a capitalization of roughly $40 billion and total sales of $480 million and negative earnings. The book value is currently 1/15th of the market value, the LT Debt position is x1.4 the equity position. The valuation of the stock is not really in line with the underlying fundamentals, to express it gently.

Add to this the large investment in Bitcoin of almost $10 billion, and we're bound to see the market value of MicroStrategy fluctuate strongly in the months to come.

Risks and Considerations

MSTY, like other income-focused options strategies, carries risks. The main risk here is the lack of downside protection. If MSTR stock drops significantly, MSTY will also lose value. While option premiums can help to mitigate losses, they don't guarantee against capital loss.

This is a reasonable risk we need to take into consideration, given the stock price of MSTR is currently valued highly based on the traditional valuation metrics (P/E, P/B, P/S) and the large investment in Bitcoin. If the stock price of MSTR comes crashing down, the value of MSTY will likely crash as well.

Moreover, MSTY’s performance is closely tied to the underlying volatility of MicroStrategy’s stock, which is in turn influenced by Bitcoin’s price movements. Given that MicroStrategy’s heavy investment in Bitcoin drives much of its stock price volatility, any downturn in the cryptocurrency market could have a direct negative impact on MSTR’s stock price and, consequently, MSTY’s income potential.

Another risk is that during periods of low volatility, the premiums collected from selling options may decrease, reducing the fund’s ability to maintain its high distribution rate. Investors in MSTY must be comfortable with the fact that the fund’s income generation is largely dependent on the level of volatility in MicroStrategy’s stock price.

Conclusion

The YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF offers an opportunity for investors to benefit from the volatility of MicroStrategy stock while receiving monthly income. The fund employs a synthetic covered call strategy, which can collect substantial income through option premiums, especially during times of high volatility.

While the upside potential in MSTR stock is limited by the fund’s options strategy, MSTY excels at delivering strong monthly distributions, which is its primary objective. Investors with a positive outlook on MicroStrategy and those comfortable with the risks associated with stock volatility and cryptocurrency exposure may find MSTY to be a valuable addition to an income-focused portfolio.

However, it’s essential to carefully consider the risks tied to MSTR’s stock volatility and its correlation with Bitcoin. MSTY can be an excellent choice for those seeking high income but with a clear understanding of the trade-offs between capital appreciation and consistent income.

data.title: Timken: Drooping Now, But Watch For Gains Again In Coming Years
data.description:
Investment thesis

The Timken Company’s (NYSE:TKR) share price has been in a trough since last May, and not surprisingly, because one of its markets, Chinese wind energy, has slowed along with the rest of China’s economy. For the past month, though, the price has been rising again.

I argue that the share price should rise in the longer-term, as the Chinese market stabilizes, a new product comes out of its Mexican bearings factory, cost controls/productivity continue to improve, and more.

Based on these strengths, I expect the share price to rise nearly 6% in the coming year and have rated it a Buy.

About Timken

The company, founded in 1899 by Henry Timken, designs, manufactures, and sells multiple product lines that involve engineered bearings and industrial motion products. All are designed to operate in severe conditions.

The engineered bearings line includes tapered, spherical, cylindrical, ball bearings, and more. Linear motion products include industrial drives, lubrication systems, belts, chains, couplings, and more.

Timken has a network of technology and engineering centers, and has many U.S. and foreign patents. However, it does not consider itself materially dependent on them.

At the close on Friday, October 18, shares traded at $87.09, and it had a market cap of $6.12 billion.

Competition and competitive advantages

In its 10-K for 2023, Timken reported that the bearing and industrial motion industries are both highly competitive. It goes on to say that it competes mainly on its “total value proposition”, which involves product design and performance, quality, price, and other factors.

It lists its two largest competitors as Sweden’s SKF Group or as it is publicly listed, AB SKF (OTCPK:SKFRY) and Germany’s SCHAEFFLER AG ADR (OTCPK:SFFLY). Online sources show the same information, naming SKF as the largest, Schaeffler second largest, and Timken third.

Of the three firms in this group, Timken has the best net margin and return on common equity or ROCE:

Except for Schaeffler’s net margin, all margins look solid for investors. They can be confident that while these companies may have their ups and downs, they all are good, long-term stocks. It may also suggest that as the largest players in the sector, their size and scope give them an edge in getting and keeping customers.

Second quarter financial results

Released on July 31, the Q2-2024 financial report shows Timken beat analysts’ estimates of EPS by $0.02 and revenue missed by 7.07% or $4.37 million. Other results included:

  • Sales of $1.18 billion, with most of the year-over-year, 7.0% decline attributed to lower energy demand in China (the Chinese economy has been growing more slowly than expected because of the property downturn and lower consumer demand).
  • Net income fell from $125.2 million or $1.73 per diluted share in the same quarter last year to $96.2 million, or $1.36 per diluted share this year.
  • Adjusted net income also fell, from $146.1 million or $2.01 per diluted share last year to $114.2 million or $1.63 per diluted share this year.

In other news, Timken took in about $232 million from selling a minority stake in Timken India Limited. And, Engineered Bearings continued to be the main breadwinner, with sales of $783.4 million, down 8.6% from last year. Industrial Motion had sales of $398.9 million, a decrease of 3.9% from the same period last year.

Looking at the consolidated balance sheet, we see cash and cash equivalents have risen from $418.9 million at the end of calendar 2023 to $469.9 million on June 30th. Short-term debt has been reduced from $605.6 million to $46.5 million in the same period. Long-term debt, though, has risen to $2.130 billion from $1.790 billion at the beginning of the year. And shareholders’ equity has risen to $2.797 billion from $2.582 billion.

Free cash flow for the second quarter slipped to $87.3 million from $94.4 million in the same period last year. However, President and CEO Richard Kyle said in the earnings release, “we expect to generate significant free cash flow over the balance of the year, which will further fuel our ability to create shareholder value through disciplined capital allocation.”

Finally, management also increased the quarterly dividend by 3% and bought back 360,000 shares; those actions added up $53.6 million delivered to shareholders.

Many investors and analysts have complained about the current slowdown, and some investors have exited the stock, pulling down the price. It peaked at $93.10 on May 15, two weeks after Timken announced its first quarter results, in which it noted the slowdown in China. The price subsequently bottomed out at $78.36 on September 6 before climbing back up again.

Unlike the pessimists, I’m bullish on the potential of Timken, especially as we’ll see in the next section, where we address its growth prospects. Regarding the Chinese slowdown, I expect it will turn around sometime in the near future and if not, this long-established company will find ways to bypass it. That may come from organic or acquisition-driven growth.

Third quarter outlook

Management expects little significant change in the third quarter (although its outlook is restricted to the second half rather than quarter by quarter. In the Q2 earnings call, CFO Fracassa provided this overview, "Organically, there are a few pluses and minuses among the sectors, but we are maintaining our outlook for organic sales to be down around 5% at the midpoint, with renewable energy still driving most of the decline, and the outlook continues to assume no recovery or inflection in the second half."

For third quarter specifics, we will turn to Earnings Whisper, which forecasts revenue of $1.14 billion. That's the same as Q3-2023 and below Q2's $1.18 billion.

The site also expects EPS of $1.37, which would be below the same quarter last year, $1.55, and the second quarter of this year, $1.63.

If these numbers turn out to be on the mark when earnings are released on Tuesday, November 5, we should expect further declines in the share price this year.

Dividends

Timken pays a dividend that currently yields 1.56%, and in absolute terms amounts to $1.36 per year. Its payout ratio is low, at 21.04%, and it has increased the dividend every year for the past 10 years.

Growth prospects

This chart of revenue, EBITDA, and net income shows how Timken has grown over the past 10 years:

There are a couple of issues that should be noted. First, the gap between revenue and EBITDA has narrowed over most of the past decade. That means the company has reduced its operating expenses or cost of goods sold, relatively speaking. In other words, management has been doing a good job of deriving more EBITDA out of each dollar of revenue.

Second, while the net income line appears relatively flat, the amount of net income has risen 111.0% (from $173.3 million in 2014 to $365.4 million (annualized) at June 30, 2024), while revenue has increased 49.68% (from $3.08 billion to $4.61 billion). Net income rising faster than revenue means that improvement between revenue and EBITDA is carrying through to the bottom line. This amounts to another management success.

CEO Kyle and CFO Philip Fracassa pointed to, in the Q2 earnings call, several positive indicators of growth in 2025 and beyond. They noted Chinese wind orders and revenue have stabilized, that the belt expansion at the bearing plant in Mexico will soon turn a cost into a revenue generator. A $600 million bond issuance, at an “attractive” rate, allowed to repay its near-term maturities and face no significant debt maturities until 2027.

These positive notes, and perhaps other factors, have led Wall Street analysts to forecast double-digit earnings increases in 2025 and 2026:

2025’s increase will help offset the loss in earnings power expected for this year, but in 2026 the company should again be a compounder.

Valuation

To put the current share price in perspective, let’s look at a 10-year chart. It shows Timken drifted along below $60 for roughly six years; then, it recovered from the supply chain crisis and has rarely slipped below $60 in the past four years:

Because of the recent price dips, the company gets good valuation grades, but not enough to make it a value stock (no A grades). This excerpt from the Seeking Alpha valuation page shows some examples:

To form a one-year price target, I will assume the share price follows earnings, as they have previously. Wall Street analysts expect earnings to fall by 13.89% this year and then gain 14.24% next year. Since the two estimates are very close, we could assume the price would remain about the same over the next 12 months.

Still, the price has been on an upswing for the past month (+6.82%), suggesting investors may look forward to 2025 rather than the rest of this year. And with the positive news we saw for 2025, I believe some increase in the price target is warranted.

So, I’m going to use the target set by Wall Street analysts, $92.27, which is a 5.95% increase over the next year. I’m using their forecast because they have been relatively accurate over the past five years:

Eight of the analysts also have a forecast for 2026, which is earnings growth of 10.10%. Combining the 2025 and 2026 estimates produces a CAGR of 35.59%. That figure, plus my long-term bullishness about the company, leads me to a Buy rating. Two other Seeking Alpha analysts have posted ratings in the past 90 days, one is a Buy and the other a Hold. The Quant system has a Hold and the Wall Street analysts have a collective Buy, based on three Strong Buys, eight Holds, and one Sell.

Risk factors

While I expect the price to rise in the next 12 months, that may turn out to be incorrect. Should the Chinese market reverse the stabilization that management reported in the earning call, sales could slow even more, pulling down earnings and ultimately the share price.

There has just been a change at the top, with Tarak Mehta replacing Richard Kyle as CEO on September 6. Mehta is new to the company, and comes from the Motion business at ABB Ltd (OTCPK:ABBNY). New leaders often inspire greater results, but there is also the chance that he may do the opposite. As Warren Buffett famously said, "I try to invest in businesses that are so wonderful that an idiot can run them. Because sooner or later, one will". Tarak Mehta Appointed President and CEO of The Timken Company

As an international company, Timken is exposed to geopolitical risks and currency risks. In particular, investors should be concerned about expanded hostilities in the Middle East, the potential for new tariffs or trade barriers, and international shipping.

To remain competitive in its businesses, the asset-heavy company will need to keep investing significant amounts of capital. If cash flow is constrained for whatever reason, it may need to reduce its returns to shareholders.

The availability of capital and debt may become more expensive because of regional or global economic conditions. That affects not only Timken, but also its customers who need to finance expensive equipment and machinery.

Conclusion

Investors who look past the short-term woes of The Timken Company should see potential for this venerable Industrials stock. As we have observed, the Chinese wind market has stabilized and several other initiatives should get earnings rising again. With that rise, the share price should also improve.

Still, investors will have to get past this year’s doldrums and look at a longer time horizon. Those who do should enjoy future capital gains and a small bonus from its growing dividend.

I have a one-year price target of $92.27 and a Buy rating.

data.title: Jane's September 2024 Retirement Income Update: Knowing When To Sell A Great Investment
data.description:
Although it may not come up very often, I think its worth discussing the concept of know when you should sell (or at least trim back) a great investment. Even the most objective investor is likely to have certain investments that are near-and-dear to their heart which can make it difficult for them to sell even when they know deep-down that the investment has run its course or is likely to underperform relative to other investment options.

In some cases this may look like a situation where there is the need to move capital from one sector (typically a sector that is performing very well) to another sector is currently underperforming but has future upside potential. I often refer to this as "reallocating" capital. Part of the reason why this often goes hand-in-hand with an investment that has performed exceptionally well is because the unrealized gains can cause an investment portfolio to be unbalanced when it grows enough over a long period of time.

Let's consider a real example:

Over the last year the information technology sector is up 49.22% while the energy sector (oil and gas) is only up .77%. If you believe that there is upside potential for the energy sector but limited upside for the information technology sector that it would make sense to sell some of the holdings in IT and use those funds to purchase holdings in the energy sector. A more practical reason for reallocating capital is if you're trying to maintain equal exposure to different sectors/industries over the long term. $10,000 invested into the IT sector one year ago would be nearly $15,000 today while $10,000 invested into the energy sector would still be approximately $10,000 today. An investor who doesn't rebalance their portfolio occasionally may be subjecting themselves to a higher level of risk that they are not aware of (the dot-com bust would be a real example of this).

But there is also another time to sell shares and that is when the stock price reaches a level where the likelihood of downside far outweighs likelihood of upside benefit. First, this does not mean that you have to sell off an entire holding (unless you want to) because the primary goal of training back shares is a lot like setting aside some money when you win at the casino. If we start gambling with $100 and we win another $100 it makes sense to set aside the $100 and continue gambling guilt-free because we know we will walk away with what we started with. Now you can imagine that there are many gamblers who would find a way to justify using the additional $100 after losing their first $100 and that eliminates the potential of a guilt-free outing because you could walk away with nothing.

NextEra Energy (NEE) is one of the stocks that we established a top-end stock price for to where we believe the likelihood of downside outweighs the likelihood of upside. What I like about this method is that the stock price is either going to go up or it's going to go down and what we primarily care about as investors is that we position our investments to capture the upside as much as possible while limiting the downside is that possible. With NEE we previously set a limit trade of $85/share for 20 shares with an open close date to allow the trade to execute any time over the next six months.

So why did we choose $85/share to trim back our holdings?

  • Over the last five years NEE has rarely traded above $85/share, and even when it has, is only been for very brief periods of time.
  • The yield that is associated with NEE is pretty minimal compared to other investments.

Limited Capital Appreciation (potential) + Low Yield = Compelling Reason To Sell

Ultimately, $85 became the guilt-free sale price that we would have no problem letting go of shares at. The transaction history below shows this.

Is worth noting that the previous trades made in May were done because it eliminated the highest-cost shares in the NEE position. After having accumulated NEE shares at a significant discount we used these trades to eliminate our higher-cost shares. In the image below you can see the realized gains were basically a break-even while the most recent transaction had a sizable gain that came with it.

What Price Would We Sell Iron Mountain?

Iron Mountain (IRM) is the most-recent stock to hit my radar with its astronomical growth over the last several years. With a cost basis in the low $30/share range and an astronomical valuation that has rapidly compressed the dividend yield sets up the same scenario of minimal capital appreciation and low dividend yield. The FastGraphs image shows just how rapidly the stock price has ascended and the P/AFFO multiple has skyrocketed.

In this situation we won't be using a limit trade because we are good with selling at $122/share. With that said, we will potentially add a limit trade to sell at an even higher price for a smaller number of shares (much like the example above with NEE). We plan to execute the sale of 100 shares (out of 235 total shares) at $122/share on 10/16/2024.

September Dividend Increases

September had a total of four holdings that increased their dividend payout or provided a special dividend.

  • Cummins (CMI) - Increased dividend by 8.3%.
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN) - Supplemental dividend of $.30/share.
  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) - Supplemental dividend of $.06/share.
  • Bank Of America (BAC) - Increased dividend by 8.3%.

Traditional IRA - September Trades

The big news with the Traditional IRA has been the influx of cash as multiple certificates of deposit which there expiration date resulting in a much larger cash stockpile. The big news is that we were able to use this cash to establish a starter position in Wisconsin Electric PDF 3.60% (OTC:WELPP).

Roth IRA - September Trades

The Roth IRA had an exceptionally busy month and there are a handful of trades to that are worth unpacking further.

Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS) - Canadian banks haven fallen out of favorability for a number of reasons and this shouldn't come as a surprise since I feel like it talk about it almost every article now. We aren't desperate to cut back BNS but if shares moved back up to $54/share we would look to cut more.

Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (RNP) - RNP has been a major holding for quite some time and will continue to be a major holding but this represented a good opportunity to cut back on high-cost shares after accumulating another 300 shares over the last 12 months with a cost basis close to $19/share on those transactions.

Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) - Just announced a 7% dividend increase but also the stock price looks attractive in the low $30/share range. We used this opportunity to establish a position especially when the energy sector prices are depressed.

Portfolio Composition

The images below are focused on what is happening now and moving forward.

The first image shows what has happened year-over-year with the portfolio in terms of which holdings are generating income.

Below is an updated table of the extremely conservative forecast that suggests income will be down -1.4% (previously -3.9%) in the Traditional IRA and up 9.2% (previously 7.6%) in the Roth IRA for FY-2024. Remember, these numbers look bleak (at least the Traditional IRA does) because it assumes that we see no dividend growth, and we do not capitalize on the additional income from CDs and Money Markets (which is something the Traditional IRA is extremely dependent on).

I have added the third table to show the combined numbers and created a new column to give a better perspective on the monthly growth or reduction of year-over-year income.

The next two images have been updated from my forecasting articles to look at how much the income has grown on a monthly basis and separate out what income is from dividends and what comes from CDs/Money Markets, etc.

This offers more insight/context as to where Jane's income is being derived (equities, fixed income, etc.). These are also more realistic numbers of what we expect to see for income growth, and also give us the ability to better track which months have the largest impact.

Here is a basic understanding of what each of the columns/fields means:

  • 2023 - Income results for 2023
  • 2024 (Ex CDs) - Estimated income 2024 (yellow) and actual income 2024 (green) but excludes all income earned from money markets, CDs, etc.
  • 2024 (W CDs) - Estimated CD & money market income 2024 (yellow) and actual income 2024 (green). Separated because we want to differentiate between dividend income and growth compared to CDs/money markets that are not subject to increases.
  • Total Income - Combined total income from dividends, CDs, Money market, etc.
  • 3%/5%/7% Increase - These columns serve as a marker so you can see how much income would need to be hit that month to achieve a specific increase. I will likely change these numbers to be more account-specific in the future (some accounts are growing more rapidly than others).

From the numbers above, we are expecting to see income growth of 4.0% in the Traditional IRA (previously 3.4%) and 13.1% in the Roth IRA (previously 12.8%) in FY-2024.

Conclusion

Something that has helped me with knowing when to sell a stock that I like is asking the right questions about the future growth/return potential. Using IRM as an example, I can ask questions when looking at different metrics.

Historical P/AFFO over the last 10 years is 12.55x compared with the current P/AFFO of 27.6x.

  • What is the likelihood of IRM continuing to grow P/AFFO at these levels based on its business model? (Ex. High, Low, etc.).
  • Is there a higher likelihood of the stock price continuing at this trajectory or that it will pullback?

Simplifying the question to say I am going to hold onto the stock because I believe there is a higher likelihood of the stock price continuing to decrease or I am going to sell shares because I believe there is a greater likelihood of downside makes the decision as clear-cut as possible.

This process is also made easier when we have a portfolio that primarily consists of dividend-paying stocks. I wouldn't necessarily apply the same methodology to a company like Amazon (AMZN) or a company that is still experiencing rapid share price appreciation.

What stocks are you seeing that aren't in Jane's Retirement portfolio? Are there any compelling investments I should be looking at? I would love to hear any ideas/suggestions in the comment section.

John and Jane are long all holdings mentioned in this article.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
